content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For years, I built applications the same way most front-end developers do: everything in pixels.
Font sizes, spacing, component dimensions, layouts — px everywhere.
Then I started leading the development of enterprise portals used by thousands of internal users across different regions, departments, and hardware setups. That’s when I discovered a problem that modern web development discussions don’t talk about enough:
Most enterprise users are not working on large 4K monitors.
Many are working on 13-inch and 14-inch corporate laptops with limited screen real estate. A portal that looks perfectly fine on a designer’s 27-inch monitor can quickly feel cramped, oversized, and frustrating on smaller screens.
My solution wasn’t more breakpoints.
It was replacing px with rem.
The Simple Idea
Instead of defining sizes directly in pixels:
.title { font-size: 24px; margin-bottom: 16px; }I started defining almost everything in rem:
.title { font-size: 2.4rem; margin-bottom: 1.6rem; }
